The Ticino Chamber of Commerce has already announced the possibility of supporting a referendum against the proposed measures if they are perceived as inadequate or unjust. For Ticino citizens, participating in public consultations and cantonal assemblies is an opportunity to influence future decisions. You can:
- Follow updates on the website of the Ticino State Council or the Chamber of Commerce.
- Attend assemblies organised by professional associations or political parties.

### 5. Explore tax alternatives

For taxpayers concerned about an increase in tax pressure, there are several strategies to optimise their position. For example:
- Diversify investments across Swiss cantons with more favourable tax rates, such as Zug or Zurich.
- Consider transferring your tax residence to a nearby canton, provided the requirements are met.
- Consult a tax advisor specialising in Ticino’s tax law to identify any potential reliefs or exemptions.


### Deadlines and procedures

The new measures will come into effect according to the following timeline:
- **2027**: Introduction of the temporary increase in the wealth tax.
- 2029: End of the temporary period. By this date, the State Council must present a definitive solution to fund the healthcare system.
- By 2029: Publication of the new rates for land registry transactions and updated tariffs for healthcare services.

To stay informed, you can subscribe to the newsletters of the Department of Finance and Economics (DFE) or the Ticino Chamber of Commerce. Additionally, the official website of the Canton of Ticino will provide all the necessary information to comply with the new regulations.


⚠️ Important: The proposed measures may undergo changes during implementation. It is advisable to periodically check the official communications from the Canton of Ticino to avoid any surprises.

Now that you have all the information, you can assess the impact of the new measures on your tax situation and plan your next steps. If you have doubts about how these changes might affect your business or investments, [check the map of cantonal tax rates](nav:tax-return) or [calculate your disposable income using our simulator](nav:payslip).

*Source: [ticinonews.ch](https://www.ticinonews.ch/ticino/leconomia-ticinese-servono-riforme-non-aggravi-fiscali-431679)*

item.title: Varese MP in Forno di Massa: “Nazi-fascist shame cannot be normalised”
item.description: Antonio Ferrara (M5S) sanctioned for blocking a remigration conference featuring far-right groups. On April 10, he commemorated the 1944 massacre in Forno, which claimed 60 lives.
item.content:encoded:
On April 10, 2026, marking the 82nd anniversary of the Liberation of the province of Massa-Carrara, Varese MP Antonio Ferrara of the Five Star Movement (M5S) traveled to Forno, a hamlet that was the site of one of the most brutal Nazi-Fascist massacres. In 1944, German troops and members of the Decima Mas (X MAS) slaughtered sixty civilians as retaliation for the partisan proclamation of the ‘Free Republic of Forno’. Ferrara attended the commemoration alongside other lawmakers who, at the end of January, had prevented a confrontation with far-right figures in Parliament.

The protest by Five Star deputies erupted after news emerged of a conference on ‘remigration’ organized by League MP Domenico Furgiele, which far-right groups were expected to attend. The event was canceled after opposition lawmakers occupied the room: 31 MPs sat or positioned themselves around the speakers’ podium to block the gathering. As a result, Ferrara and 21 colleagues received a five-day suspension from the Chamber of Deputies’ presidency, while ten other MPs were sanctioned with a four-day suspension for filling all available seats.

During the commemoration in Forno, Ferrara spoke alongside Riccardo Ricciardi, the M5S group leader in the Chamber, delivering a scathing critique of the politicization of historical memory. ‘There are places where memory cannot be treated as folklore or reduced to a PR stunt. Forno is one of those places,’ he stated. The MP emphasized that ‘here, we are not recalling abstractions, but facts, responsibilities, blood, and victims.’ He also warned of ‘a growing climate of normalization for nostalgic and neo-Fascist circles, even within public and institutional debate.’

### The Forno massacre: a slaughter without justice

On September 13, 1944, after days of partisan resistance, German forces and Decima Mas militiamen broke through Forno’s defenses. House-to-house raids followed. German soldiers and Decima Mas fascists executed sixty civilians, including women and the elderly, in one of the war’s most brutal reprisals. The village was razed to the ground, and only a handful of survivors managed to flee into the woods. For decades, the massacre was forgotten until a commemorative plaque in 2003 reignited collective memory.

Ferrara stressed that ‘the Decima Mas is not a culture to exhume: it is a shame to remember.’ His remarks come amid an increasingly heated debate in Italy over the public use of history and the presence of Fascist-era symbols and references within institutions.

### Institutional reactions and controversies

The suspensions of Ferrara and his colleagues sparked cross-party controversy. While the Five Star Movement defended the move as opposition to ‘dangerous ideologies,’ the League condemned it as an ‘attack on freedom of thought.’ League MP Furgiele, the organizer of the canceled conference, accused the Five Star deputies of ‘censoring democratic debate.’

Meanwhile, in Forno, the commemoration was seen as an act of redemption. ‘This is a place where memory must remain alive, not a political battleground,’ declared a representative of the local ANPI (National Association of Italian Partisans). The presence of Ferrara and other MPs shone a light on a dark chapter of Italian history, often overlooked in school textbooks and public discourse.

> 📊 **Key takeaway**: The Forno massacre remains a stark reminder of the brutality of Nazi-Fascist repression, yet its legacy continues to fuel debates over memory, justice, and the boundaries of free speech in Italy.

> ⚠️ Context: The controversy over the canceled ‘remigration’ conference highlights rising tensions over far-right influence in Italian politics and institutions. The events in Ferrara and the Forno massacre spark broader reflections on the role of historical memory in contemporary society, particularly in a context like Ticino, where daily coexistence among diverse cultures and histories is the norm. For cross-border workers who cross the border between Lombardy and Ticino every day, this episode serves as a reminder of how the past can resurface unexpectedly, even decades later.

### Historical memory: a tool for cohesion or division?

The commemoration of Forno coincides with a period in Europe marked by the resurgence of political movements claiming ideologies tied to fascism or Nazism. In Italy, the 1952 Scelba Law prohibits the reconstitution of the Fascist Party, yet the presence of symbols and references to that era in public discourse remains a subject of debate. Ferrara’s actions underscore how certain themes cannot be treated lightly, especially in a country founded on an antifascist Constitution.

For cross-border workers in Ticino, this discussion takes on particular significance. Canton Ticino, with its history of neutrality and openness, stands at the crossroads of cultures where historical memory can serve as both a unifying and divisive force. The presence of generations-old Italian and Swiss communities living side by side makes the reflection on these themes even more urgent.

### Impact on Ticino’s political debate

While Ferrara’s case primarily concerns Italian politics, its echoes have been felt beyond national borders. In Ticino, where immigration and identity are often at the heart of public debate, discussions on how to manage historical memory take on added relevance. Canton Ticino, long committed to upholding human rights, may be called upon to take a stand on similar initiatives involving symbols or ideologies linked to fascism.

The controversy over ‘remigration’ and the legitimization of far-right environments in Italian politics raises questions for Switzerland as well. On one hand, the country has a tradition of neutrality and respect for diversity, but on the other, the presence of foreign communities and coexistence with different cultures demand constant vigilance against the risks of extremist trends. In this context, historical memory becomes a fundamental tool for preventing the repetition of past mistakes.

> 📊 Key takeaway: Historical memory is not just about the past—it is a living tool that shapes our present and future, especially in multicultural societies like Ticino.

### What changes for cross-border workers?

From a practical standpoint, Ferrara’s case does not immediately alter the situation for cross-border workers in Ticino. However, it sends an important signal about how Italian political debates can influence the local context. For transborder commuters who cross the border daily, historical awareness becomes an element of safeguarding their own identity and the values underpinning coexistence between Italy and Switzerland.

Moreover, the discussion on historical memory could lead to awareness-raising initiatives in Ticino’s schools or public events involving cross-border workers. These moments could provide opportunities to strengthen dialogue between communities and promote a shared culture of remembrance.

> ⚠️ Food for thought: Cross-border workers are not just witnesses to history—they are active participants in shaping a future where memory serves as a bridge, not a barrier.

💡 Further reading:
- [The Scelba Law and its legacy](nav:scelba_law)
- [Ticino’s approach to multiculturalism](nav:ticino_multiculturalism)
- [Cross-border commuting: challenges and opportunities](nav:cross_border_challenges)

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). For cross-border workers interested in exploring the theme of historical memory and its impact on Ticinese society, there are several tools and initiatives that can be helpful. Here’s a practical guide to get you started.

## How to participate in historical memory initiatives in Ticino

The Canton of Ticino regularly promotes events, conferences, and exhibitions on the topic of historical memory, particularly around significant anniversaries such as Holocaust Remembrance Day (January 27) or Swiss National Day (August 1). To stay informed, you can consult the event calendar on the [Department of Education, Culture, and Sport of the Canton of Ticino](nav:calendar) website.

Many initiatives are organized in collaboration with local associations, schools, and cultural centers. For example, the Cantonal Museum of History in Lugano frequently hosts temporary exhibitions on themes related to World War II and its consequences in Ticino. Public libraries, such as those in Bellinzona or Locarno, also organize meetings with historians and witnesses to discuss these topics.

> 📊 **Did you know?** The Cantonal Museum of History in Lugano offers guided tours in multiple languages, including English, making it accessible to cross-border workers.

## Tools to deepen local history

For those wanting to explore the history of Forno or the Nazi-Fascist massacres in Italy, there are several online resources available. The [Istituto Nazionale Ferruccio Parri](https://www.istitutoparri.it) website provides historical resources and archival documents on the massacres of World War II. The Wikipedia page on the [Forno massacre](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eccidio_di_Forno) also offers an updated overview with links to primary sources.

In Ticino, the State Archives preserve historical documents that can help understand how the memory of these events has been locally managed. For example, you can consult the minutes of the Ticino State Council from the 1940s to see how local authorities addressed the issue of refugees and massacres during the war.

## What to do if you encounter fascist or Nazi-related speech or symbols

If, during your work or daily life, you encounter speeches or symbols that evoke fascist or Nazi ideologies, it’s important to report them to the competent authorities. In Ticino, the Cantonal Police and the Department of Health and Social Affairs are the bodies responsible for monitoring such phenomena.

You can also report such incidents to local associations dedicated to historical memory, such as the Ticino Association for Peace and Human Rights. These organizations often run awareness campaigns and can provide support in case of a formal complaint.

> ⚠️ Important: Reporting such incidents helps combat hate speech and promotes a culture of remembrance and respect.

## Resources for cross-border workers

For cross-border workers who wish to actively participate in historical memory initiatives, there are several volunteering opportunities available. For example, the Swiss Association for Human Rights offers training programs for those interested in deepening their knowledge of memory and anti-fascism themes. The International Civil Service also organizes projects in Ticino that involve young people and adults in activities related to the protection of historical memory.

Finally, for those who wish to discuss these topics with other cross-border workers, you can join discussion groups organized by the Ticino Documentation Center. These meetings provide an opportunity to reflect on how historical memory can be a valuable asset in fostering coexistence among diverse cultures.

To explore how historical memory and values of coexistence can influence your experience as a cross-border worker, try our [social impact calculator](nav:livability).

*Source: [varesenoi.it](https://www.varesenoi.it/2026/04/16/leggi-notizia/argomenti/politica-20/articolo/il-deputato-varesino-ferrara-nel-luogo-della-strage-nazifascista-la-vergogna-non-si-sdogana-1.html)*

item.title: Deprexis to be reimbursed by health insurers: what changes for cross-border workers and Ticino
item.description: From 1 July 2026, the Deprexis app will be prescribable by doctors and reimbursed by health insurers. A digital tool for mild to moderate depression, but with limitations and doubts about its actual effectiveness.
item.content:encoded:
From **1 July 2026**, Swiss doctors will be able to prescribe the Deprexis app, a digital psychotherapy platform that will be fully reimbursed by health insurance funds. This is the first application of its kind authorised by the Federal Office of Public Health (FOPH) and intended for adult patients with mild to moderate depressive disorders. The introduction is no coincidence: according to an evaluation by the University of Bern, only half of people with mental health problems seek medical advice, and among them, only half manage to find a specialist due to long waiting lists.

The app, developed in Germany, is designed as a complementary tool to traditional psychotherapy, not as a replacement. The programme is based on pre-programmed algorithms that provide personalised advice based on the patient’s symptoms. Available features include exercises to counter negative thoughts, emotional strengthening techniques and the option to keep a mood diary. According to Professor Chantal Martin Sölch of the University of Fribourg, 'Numerous studies conducted in different contexts and countries show that this application genuinely reduces depressive symptoms.' Its effectiveness increases further when used alongside traditional therapy, also improving the latter’s outcomes.

### Why Switzerland needs this tool

The issue of access to psychological care in Switzerland has long been known. Waiting times for a psychotherapist can exceed six months, and for cross-border workers employed in Ticino but living in Lombardy, the problem is even greater. Often, these workers find themselves unable to secure a timely appointment in their place of residence due to high demand or a lack of specialists. In this context, the Deprexis app represents an attempt to fill a gap in the healthcare system by offering an immediate and accessible alternative.

The FOPH has emphasised that the app was authorised following rigorous scientific evaluation, but has also specified that it is not suitable for crisis situations, such as suicidal thoughts. Dr Caterina Ilario of the Geneva University Hospitals (HUG) expressed doubts about its long-term usefulness: 'A depressed patient is often unmotivated and feels shame. The risk is that they will abandon the tool much more easily than face-to-face therapy.' Despite these criticisms, the Swiss Federation of Psychologists (FSP) has welcomed the innovation, describing it as 'a good start', but stressed that Switzerland must still invest more in innovative digital solutions for mental health.

### How it works in practice: what changes from July 2026

For patients, the process will be as follows:

1. Medical prescription: Only a doctor will be able to prescribe the application after assessing the patient’s symptoms.
2. App access: Once prescribed, the health insurance fund will reimburse the cost of the subscription (amount not yet specified).
3. Use: The app will provide pre-programmed content, without generative artificial intelligence. Patients will be able to access exercises, advice and a mood diary.
4. Integration with traditional therapy: The aim is for the app to be used between sessions or while waiting for an appointment with a specialist.

### Expert reactions: between optimism and scepticism

- In favour: The FSP believes Switzerland is lagging behind other countries in the development of digital tools for mental health. 'Psychological research constantly produces new tools, but Switzerland must do more to encourage these innovations,' says the organisation’s spokesperson.

- Criticisms: Some professionals, such as Dr Ilario, point out that empathy and human interaction are fundamental in psychotherapy and that an app cannot replace them. The risk, they argue, is that users will abandon the tool before achieving concrete results.

### The Ticino case: an opportunity for cross-border workers

For cross-border workers employed in Ticino but living in Lombardy, the introduction of Deprexis could represent a temporary solution while waiting for an appointment with a Swiss psychotherapist. The problem of waiting lists is particularly acute in Ticino, where demand for psychological services is high both among residents and cross-border commuters. Furthermore, for those experiencing psychological distress but who do not wish or are unable to see a therapist in person, the app offers an anonymous and accessible alternative.

> 📊 Key figures
> - Only half of people with mental health problems seek medical advice.
> - Waiting times for a psychotherapist can exceed six months in Switzerland.
> - Deprexis is the first digital psychotherapy app reimbursed by Swiss health insurance funds.

> 💡 Did you know?
> Studies show that combining digital tools like Deprexis with traditional therapy improves outcomes for patients with mild to moderate depression.

> ⚠️ Limitations
> - The app is not suitable for crisis situations, such as suicidal thoughts.
> - It should not replace face-to-face therapy but serve as a complementary tool. The introduction of Deprexis into the Swiss healthcare system raises practical questions for cross-border workers and Ticino residents. While the app does not replace traditional therapy, it represents a **tangible shift** in how mental health is addressed. But what does this mean in concrete terms for those who live and work between Italy and Switzerland?

### Before vs after: what changes for patients

Before July 2026, those suffering from mild or moderate depression had few options:
- Turning to a private psychotherapist, with high costs and long waiting lists.
- Waiting months for an appointment in the public service.
- Not seeking help at all, due to fear of social stigma or lack of motivation.

After July 2026, the option of a digital application becomes accessible, but with clear limitations:
- It is not a replacement therapy, but a complementary tool.
- It is not suitable for severe cases or emergency situations (e.g., suicidal thoughts).
- It requires a medical prescription, so it cannot be downloaded independently.

### How it integrates with the Ticino healthcare system

The Canton of Ticino, like the rest of Switzerland, is facing a crisis in access to psychological care. According to FOPH data, only 50% of people with mental health issues seek consultation, and of these, only half find a specialist available. In this context, Deprexis could represent a piece of a broader strategy, but not the definitive solution.

However, for cross-border workers employed in Lugano, Bellinzona, or Locarno but residing in the provinces of Como or Varese, the app offers some advantages:
- Anonymity: Those who fear social stigma in Italy can use the tool without having to visit a Swiss practice.
- Accessibility: The app is available at any time, without the need to travel.
- Zero cost: As it is reimbursed by health insurance, it does not burden the family budget.

📊 Potential and limits
The app could help reduce the mental health care gap, but it is not a magic solution. Its effectiveness depends on user compliance and the ability to integrate it with traditional therapies when necessary.

### Doubts and risks: what psychologists say

Despite its potential, many professionals remain skeptical. Dr. Caterina Ilario of HUG noted that 'the dropout rate could be higher than with in-person therapy.' This is because depressed patients often lack motivation and may abandon the app after just a few uses. Furthermore, the absence of a human relationship makes it difficult to build the trust necessary for an effective therapeutic journey.

Another critical point concerns data privacy. The FOPH has specified that information security is guaranteed, but no details have been provided on how sensitive user data is managed. For a cross-border worker, this could be a hurdle, especially if they live in Italy and fear that information could be shared with local authorities.

### Comparison with other digital tools

Deprexis is not the only digital psychotherapy app available in Switzerland. In recent years, numerous 'do-it-yourself' platforms have emerged, promising psychological support but often without rigorous scientific validation. The FOPH has clarified that only Deprexis has been authorized after a validation process, while other tools may not offer the same guarantees of efficacy and safety.

⚠️ Attention to unregulated apps
Many apps claim to provide mental health support but lack medical oversight. For cross-border workers, it is essential to rely on FOPH-approved tools to ensure safety and effectiveness.

### A useful tool, but not a solution

Deprexis represents a step forward in making mental health support more accessible, but it is not a cure-all. Its success will depend on how it is integrated into the broader healthcare system and on users' willingness to engage with it consistently. For now, it remains a complementary tool—one that could make a difference, but not replace traditional care when needed.

In summary, Deprexis marks a step forward in efforts to improve access to psychological care in Switzerland, but it is not a magic solution. For cross-border workers, it could serve as temporary assistance while waiting for an appointment with a specialist, yet it cannot replace the human connection and personalization of traditional therapy.

### Comparative tables: before and after Deprexis

| Aspect | Before July 2026 | After July 2026 |
|----------------------|-----------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| Access to care | Long waiting lists, high private costs | Prescribable digital tool fully reimbursed |
| Prescription | Only for traditional therapy | Required for Deprexis |
| Cost | Variable (private: 150-250 CHF/session) | Free (fully reimbursed by health insurance) |
| Effectiveness | Depends on specialist availability | Complementary, with proven results |
| Privacy | Managed by the therapist | Managed by the app (details not yet specified) |
| Suitable for severe cases | No | No (only mild/moderate cases) |

### Who can and cannot use Deprexis

- Can use the app:
 - Adults with mild or moderate depression.
 - Patients awaiting an appointment with a psychotherapist.
 - Those already in traditional therapy wishing to supplement with digital exercises.
- Cannot use the app:
 - Minors.
 - Patients with severe depression or suicidal thoughts.
 - Those without a medical prescription.

### The role of Ticino’s health insurance providers

Health insurance companies operating in Ticino will need to adjust their insurance plans to include reimbursement for Deprexis. It remains unclear whether all insurers will offer the same level of coverage or if there will be variations in costs for users. However, the Federal Office of Public Health (UFSP) has specified that the application will be fully reimbursed, with no deductibles or out-of-pocket expenses for patients.

📊 A word of caution

For the Canton of Ticino, where demand for psychological services is high among both residents and cross-border workers, Deprexis could prove a useful tool, though not a definitive solution. The app may ease pressure on psychotherapists’ waiting lists but does not eliminate the need for a more efficient and accessible healthcare system.

Moreover, for cross-border workers living in Lombardy but working in Ticino, the application offers an anonymous and immediate alternative, eliminating the need to visit a Swiss practice. However, doubts remain about its effectiveness for those suffering from severe or chronic psychological issues.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). From 1 July 2026, cross-border workers and Ticino residents suffering from mild or moderate depression will be able to access Deprexis, a digitally reimbursed psychotherapy app provided by health insurance funds. But how do you access the tool? What steps need to be taken? And what concrete changes will this bring to everyday life?

## Step 1: Obtain a medical prescription

The first step is to consult a doctor in Ticino. Only a doctor, after assessing the symptoms, can prescribe the app. This means it will not be possible to download Deprexis independently, even if paying out of pocket. For cross-border workers without a Ticino-based GP, the alternative is to consult a private specialist or visit a public outpatient clinic.

## Step 2: Download the app and activate reimbursement

Once the prescription is obtained, the patient must:
- Download the Deprexis app from the official store (Google Play or Apple App Store).
- Register using their personal details.
- Enter the prescription code provided by the doctor.
- Wait for confirmation of activation from the health insurance fund.

The Federal Office of Public Health (FOPH) has specified that the subscription cost will be fully reimbursed, but the exact amount and details of the reimbursement process have not yet been announced. It is advisable to contact your health insurance fund to check for any additional steps.

## Step 3: Use the app correctly

Once activated, the app will provide a series of exercises and tailored advice based on the symptoms reported by the patient. Here’s how to maximise the benefits:
- Regularly complete the mood diary to monitor progress.
- Perform the suggested exercises at least 3-4 times a week.
- Avoid discontinuing use after the first few days, even if results are not immediate.

However, it is important to remember that Deprexis is not a replacement for traditional therapy. The app is designed as a complementary support tool, to be used between sessions or while waiting for an appointment with a psychotherapist.

> ⚠️ **Important note**: If symptoms worsen or persist, always consult a healthcare professional.

## What to do if the app doesn’t work or you have doubts

If technical issues arise or you have questions about how to proceed, patients can:
- Contact Deprexis technical support via the app itself.
- Speak with the prescribing doctor for guidance.
- Contact their health insurance fund for assistance regarding reimbursement and usage.

## Deadlines and timelines

The introduction date is set for 1 July 2026, but it is not yet clear whether there will be differences between the various Ticino health insurance funds. It is recommended to:
- Check with your health insurance fund to confirm whether the app is included in your insurance plan.
- Book an appointment with your doctor in advance to avoid waiting lists.
- Stay informed through official FOPH or FSP channels for any updates.

## Frequently asked questions: what to know before starting

### Who can access Deprexis?

The app is available to Ticino residents and cross-border workers with mild or moderate depression who have a medical prescription. It is not intended for severe cases or emergencies.

### How long does a subscription last?

The duration of the subscription will be determined by the prescribing doctor, based on the patient’s needs. The FOPH has not yet specified a standard duration.

### Is the app available in multiple languages?

Yes, Deprexis is available in several languages, including Italian, German, French, and English.

### Can I use Deprexis without a prescription?

No. A medical prescription is mandatory for reimbursement and access to the app.

### What happens if I lose my prescription code?

Contact your prescribing doctor or health insurance fund to request a new code.

### Is my data protected?

Yes. Deprexis complies with Swiss and European data protection regulations. All patient data is encrypted and stored securely.

### Can I use Deprexis alongside other therapies?

Yes. The app is designed to complement traditional therapy, not replace it. Always inform your therapist if you are using Deprexis.


📊 Key takeaways:
- Deprexis is a digital psychotherapy tool for mild to moderate depression.
- A medical prescription is required for reimbursement and access.
- The app provides personalised exercises and progress tracking.
- It is not a substitute for traditional therapy but serves as a supportive tool.
- Technical support and reimbursement details should be confirmed with your health insurance fund.

item.title: Varese 2026: Swiss-Italy Congress on Taxation and Cross-Border Work
item.description: Taxation, cryptocurrencies, and real estate: the Swiss-Italy Congress in Varese examines the latest developments for cross-border workers and businesses, with 1.7 million tourist arrivals recorded in 2025.
item.content:encoded:
📊 The 10th Swiss-Italy Congress, organized by Studio Giallo at the Ville Ponti in Varese, brought together experts, businesses, and cross-border workers to tackle the challenges of employment and investments across borders. Key topics included taxation, cryptocurrencies, and the mobility of assets between Italy and Ticino.

### Growing tourism between Lombardy and the Canton of Ticino
Mauro Vitiello, President of the Varese Chamber of Commerce, opened the event by presenting 2025 data: 1.7 million tourist arrivals and 3.2 million overnight stays, both up by 10% compared to 2024. The Varese Welcome Foundation, involving over 30 municipalities, aims to coordinate territorial promotion with initiatives such as the **Lakes Cycle Path**, designed for both tourism and daily mobility. The project strengthens ties with Ticino, where daily cross-border connections are becoming increasingly frequent.

Vitiello also highlighted a shift in residency choices: 'We have received requests from citizens working in Switzerland who want to relocate to Italy due to rising living costs.' This trend is reshaping the balance between the two countries, historically linked by strong labor interconnections.

### Focus on cross-border taxation and assets
The heart of the conference focused on tax obligations for those holding assets abroad. Salvatore Giallo, an international taxation expert, reminded attendees that Italian regulations require financial monitoring in the RW framework for foreign assets, with penalties up to 15% of the undeclared value. 'Monitoring costs little, but not doing so can cost dearly,' Giallo warned, citing automatic information exchanges between states that make it increasingly difficult to hide undeclared assets.

Particular attention was given to safety deposit boxes, often considered opaque but which, according to Giallo, 'are increasingly traceable thanks to targeted controls.' Capital gains on foreign real estate are also subject to reporting obligations, with taxation varying according to local regulations.

### Cryptocurrencies: stricter rules in Italy and Switzerland
Andrea Puzone and Pietro Soldati analyzed cryptocurrency taxation in Italy and Switzerland. In Italy, from 2026, the capital gains tax rate on many cryptocurrencies has risen to 33%, up from the previous 26%. Financial monitoring is also mandatory, with penalties up to 15% for omitted declarations. From September 2027, the OECD’s Common Reporting Standard (CRS) will come into force, increasing the traceability of international transactions.

In Switzerland, private capital gains remain tax-exempt only if the activity is not considered professional. This is a competitive advantage for cross-border workers investing in cryptocurrencies, but it requires careful attention to local regulations to avoid disputes.

### Controls and borders: the challenge for the Guardia di Finanza
Francesca Bertone, Lieutenant Colonel of the Guardia di Finanza, outlined control activities on the Italian-Swiss border, one of Europe’s most dynamic with over 200,000 daily crossings. Bertone stressed that 'tax fraud does not respect borders,' and collaboration between Italy and Switzerland is crucial to combating evasion. Key issues include cracking down on currency smuggling and suspicious capital movements, particularly near crossings like Brogeda and Ponte Chiasso.

### Real estate investments: what changes for cross-border workers
Another central topic was cross-border property purchases. Experts highlighted how Italian regulations impose an obligation to declare foreign real estate, with taxation both in Italy and in the country of residence. In Switzerland, taxation depends on the canton and type of property, with rates varying significantly between Ticino and other regions.

The complexity of regulations and the need to avoid double taxation have led many cross-border workers to seek specialized advice, especially for purchase-sale transactions or inheritance matters. [object Object]

[object Object]

## Useful planning tools
To estimate your pension strategy, use the [pension planner](nav:pension) and the [pillar 3 simulator](nav:pillar3). [object Object]

#### 4. Changing your tax residence: what to do if you move to Italy
If you're considering relocating to Italy to reduce your cost of living, follow these steps:

Before the move:
1. Notify the Ticino municipality of your departure and the Italian Revenue Agency (Agenzia delle Entrate).
2. Declare your foreign assets in the Italian tax return (730/Redditi PF) by the deadline.
3. Check local taxes in Ticino: some taxes (such as the wealth tax) may still apply until the day of your move.

After the move:
1. Update your details with the Italian Revenue Agency and the Italian National Institute for Social Security (INPS).
2. Verify the taxation of income earned in Switzerland: in Italy, foreign income is taxed according to Italian rates, with a possible tax credit for taxes already paid in Ticino.
3. Check for repatriation benefits (e.g., the repatriation tax relief if you return after at least 2 years of living abroad).

⚠️ Important: If you maintain your tax residence in Switzerland but work in Ticino, you may be subject to taxation in both Italy and Switzerland. Check the Double Taxation Agreement between the two countries.

#### 5. Where to find help and useful tools
- Accountants and tax advisors: Consult professionals specializing in international taxation, especially for complex assets or cryptocurrency investments.
- Italian Revenue Agency: On their website [agenziaentrate.gov.it](https://www.agenziaentrate.gov.it), you’ll find guides and tax return forms.
- SUPSI/USI: The Università della Svizzera Italiana offers courses and seminars on taxation and cross-border work.
- Guardia di Finanza: To report anomalies or clarify tax audit procedures, contact their local offices in Lombardy.

📌 Key deadlines to remember:
- Italian tax return: End of November (730 form) or end of June (Redditi PF form).
- RW section: Mandatory completion by the tax return deadline.
- CARF Protocol: From September 2027, greater transparency for international transactions.

### Final call to action: act now to avoid surprises
Don’t wait until the last moment: the complexity of cross-border tax regulations requires time to manage properly. Whether you're a cross-border worker with assets in Switzerland, a cryptocurrency investor, or a citizen considering a move to Italy, the most common mistake is underestimating tax obligations.

💡 What to do today:
1. Conduct a tax health check of your assets and foreign investments.
2. Consult an accountant to verify your tax position.
3. Use Frontaliere Ticino tools to calculate taxes and simulate scenarios:
 - [Tax return calculator for cross-border workers](nav:tax-return)
 - [Cost of living comparison: Ticino vs Italy](nav:cost-of-living)
 - [Guide to residence permits for cross-border workers](nav:permit-compare)

Don’t risk penalties or audits: tax evasion doesn’t pay, but proper planning does.

*Source: [varesenews.it](https://www.varesenews.it/2026/04/al-congresso-svizzera-italia-a-varese-focus-su-fisco-lavoro-e-investimenti-oltre-confine/2549716/)*

item.title: Statue ‘Return to Nature’ in Chiasso: petition stalled for a year
item.description: The petition to remove the statue ‘Return to Nature’ in Chiasso, signed by 433 people in May 2025, is still awaiting an official response from the Municipal Council. Municipal councillor Francesco Romano (HelvEthica Ticino) is calling for clarity.
item.content:encoded:
It was May 23, 2025, when 433 residents of Chiasso submitted a petition to the town hall, calling for the removal of the statue titled **‘Return to Nature’**, located in a public space in the city. Nearly a year later, the petition has still not received an official response from the municipal administration. This is the complaint raised by municipal councillor Francesco Romano, representative of the HelvEthica Ticino association, who in an official question to the Chiasso town hall highlighted several critical points regarding the months-long silence.

In the text of the question, Romano explicitly asks the Chiasso town hall to provide a formal response to the petition’s promoters, noting how the delay in responding 'raises questions about administrative timeliness, transparency, and respect for civic participation.' The association is not merely seeking explanations: it wants to know why no response has been provided, which office or department is handling the matter, and, above all, whether the town hall intends to communicate a clear position on the petition itself.

### A divided petition

The statue ‘Return to Nature’, by an artist not specified in the source, has over the years become a reference point for certain segments of the population who view it as an example of public art that clashes with the urban context. Among the petition’s promoters are those who believe the work, located in an area frequented by families and children, may convey messages inconsistent with the environmental and social values of the local community. However, the call for removal is not universally supported: some consider it an art piece worth preserving, while others appreciate its ecological symbolism.

The Chiasso town hall, contacted by theRegione, has yet to provide a public response or an update on the matter. The administrative silence risks fueling further controversy, particularly among petition signatories, who feel ignored after months of waiting.

### Timelines and procedures: when will a response arrive?

In his remarks, councillor Romano asks the town hall to clarify within what timeframe a formal response will be provided, specifying whether the petition will be accepted, rejected, or alternative solutions will be considered. The association also calls for the introduction of organizational measures to ensure that, in the future, petitions receive a formal response within reasonable deadlines. To date, it remains unclear whether the matter has been assigned to a specific office or is still in the preliminary evaluation phase.

The case raises broader questions about how civic participation functions in Chiasso and the relationship between the municipal administration and residents. The delay in responding could indeed impact residents’ trust in local institutions, especially at a time when the city is preparing to discuss strategic issues such as urban redevelopment and environmental sustainability.

> 📊 Key takeaway: The prolonged silence from the Chiasso town hall on the petition has sparked debates about transparency and civic engagement, with residents questioning the administration’s commitment to addressing their concerns.

> ⚠️ Food for thought: How can municipalities balance artistic expression with community values while ensuring timely responses to civic petitions?

> 💡 Behind the scenes: The statue’s ambiguous symbolism has divided opinions, reflecting deeper tensions between tradition, modernity, and environmental awareness in urban spaces. The case of the petition regarding the ‘Ritorno alla natura’ statue in Chiasso is a prime example of how civic participation can clash with the pace and procedures of Switzerland’s administrative machinery. For those who live or work in Chiasso, this situation is not merely a matter of principle but can have practical repercussions on how local decision-making processes are managed. Here’s what you need to know.

### The value of civic participation in Chiasso

Chiasso, a border city with over 7,000 cross-border workers crossing the Brogeda checkpoint daily to commute to work in Ticino, is a socially and politically dynamic hub. The petition about the statue is just one of many instances where citizens demand to be heard on issues affecting the city’s livability. However, when responses are delayed, the risk is generating frustration and disillusionment toward institutions.

Councillor Francesco Romano highlighted in his statement how the administration’s silence on the statue case could undermine the credibility of the Municipal Council. ‘Administrative promptness is not an option but a necessity to maintain citizens’ trust,’ he emphasized in a press release. For a cross-border worker commuting to Chiasso daily for work or services, this situation can be frustrating, especially considering that the city serves as a reference point for thousands of people living between Italy and Switzerland.

### What changes for cross-border workers?

For cross-border workers transiting through Chiasso daily, the statue and petition case carries particular significance. The city is a crossroads of cultures, languages, and habits, and managing social conflicts becomes crucial for territorial cohesion. While some demand the removal of the artwork for ethical or aesthetic reasons, others see it as an integral part of the urban identity.

In this context, the Municipality’s delayed response could be interpreted as a sign of disinterest toward the demands of both residents and cross-border workers. According to unspecified local sources, the lack of a formal reply could also slow down the launch of other urban redevelopment projects, creating a domino effect on initiatives directly involving the population.

### Possible scenarios: what could happen in the coming months?

The current situation leaves room for several scenarios. The first possibility is that the Chiasso Municipal Council decides to provide a swift response, perhaps rejecting the petition or proposing a mediation. In this case, the matter would be resolved quickly, but the damage to trust could already be done.

A second scenario suggests the process could be further postponed, possibly pending additional evaluations or external consultations. In this case, the petition organizers might decide to bring the issue to the attention of local media or organize public initiatives to demand a clear stance.

Finally, there’s the chance the Municipality will partially accept the petition, for example, by initiating a dialogue with citizens or commissioning an artistic and social evaluation of the statue. If managed transparently, this approach could represent a constructive solution for all stakeholders involved.

### The role of civic associations

Associations like HelvEthica Ticino play a key role in urging institutions to respect the timelines of democratic participation. For cross-border workers, who often face complex procedures to secure work or social rights, adherence to administrative deadlines is fundamental to avoid uncertainty.

In this case, Romano’s intervention could mark a first step toward greater attention from the Municipality toward citizens’ demands. However, it will be essential to closely monitor developments over the coming months to determine whether the matter concludes with a concrete response or continues to drag on.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). The petition calling for the removal of the Ritorno alla natura statue in Chiasso has yet to receive an official response after nearly a year. If you are among the signatories or simply wish to follow the developments of this case, here’s what you can do and what the next steps are.


### How to check the status of the petition?

The first step for those who signed the petition is to contact HelvEthica Ticino directly, the association that has been overseeing the matter since the beginning. You can find their contact details on the association’s website or through their official social media channels. Alternatively, you can send a formal request to the Municipality of Chiasso via:

- **Official email**: using the Municipality of Chiasso’s official email address (not specified in the source).
- PEC: if you have a certified email address, you can submit a formal request.
- Municipal office: by visiting the Municipality’s offices in person to inquire about the petition’s status.


### What if no response is received?

If no formal response is forthcoming in the coming months, the petition’s promoters may consider taking further action, such as:

- Organizing an additional signature drive: to increase pressure on the administration and demonstrate the growing public interest in the issue.
- Engaging local media: to bring the matter to public attention and prompt a response from the authorities.
- Filing a formal complaint: with the Ticino Administrative Court if the lack of response constitutes a violation of administrative procedures.


### Useful resources for cross-border workers

For those who cross the border daily for work, this case offers an opportunity to reflect on the importance of civic participation, even in a transborder context. If you are interested in similar initiatives or wish to stay updated on topics related to the livability of Chiasso, you can consult:

- The Municipality of Chiasso’s website: for any official updates on the petition.
- HelvEthica Ticino’s social media pages: to follow developments and participate in any initiatives.
- Local discussion forums: such as those on laRegione or other Ticino-based news portals.


> The Ritorno alla natura statue case highlights a broader issue: the need for transparency and promptness from local administrations, particularly in a city like Chiasso that serves as a meeting point for diverse cultures and customs. For cross-border workers, who experience the realities of the border every day, adherence to procedures and active participation are key to building a cohesive and respectful community.


If you are interested in contributing to the discussion or simply wish to stay informed, we invite you to follow the official channels and participate in any initiatives that may be organized in the coming months. Your voice matters, especially in a city that stands at the crossroads between Italy and Switzerland.

📊 Want to know how civic participation works in Ticino? Read our guide on [how to submit a petition to the Municipality](nav:whatif).

Compare the [CHF/EUR exchange rate](nav:exchange) in real time across providers: save up to 1.5% on monthly transfer fees.

*Source: [laregione.ch](https://www.laregione.ch/cantone/mendrisiotto/1918690/ritorno-alla-natura-petizione-ancora-inevasa-a-chiasso)*

item.title: Chiasso seeks solutions to hire more residents: what changes for cross-border workers and businesses
item.description: Chiasso councillor Francesco Romano has asked the Municipal Council to identify concrete tools that can encourage companies to prioritise hiring Ticinese residents over cross-border workers.
item.content:encoded:
On Thursday, April 16, 2026, Chiasso municipal councillor Francesco Romano of HelvEthica Ticino submitted a formal question to the Chiasso Municipal Council urging the search for tools to encourage the hiring of local residents by local businesses. According to Romano, a higher proportion of residents among workers in companies operating in the municipality could strengthen the tax revenue from individuals domiciled in Chiasso while also reinforcing the local economic and social fabric.

In his request, the councillor highlights that the Municipality lacks selective tax relief tools for individual companies based on the number of resident employees, as this area is heavily regulated by higher-level law. Romano therefore asks the Municipal Council to specify which non-tax measures could be concretely implemented to incentivize businesses to prioritize local residents. Among the proposals put forward are municipal contributions to training, incentives for resident apprentices, measures to support professional integration, and concessions on municipal services or fees.

The question also focuses on promoting criteria related to vocational training, apprenticeships, and corporate social responsibility. Finally, Romano asks the Council to identify the main legal, administrative, or financial obstacles currently preventing the Municipality from adopting more effective measures to support local employment.

### The regulatory context: what Chiasso can and cannot do

The Chiasso Municipal Council operates within a fairly rigid regulatory framework. As highlighted in the question, federal and cantonal law severely restricts the possibility of using direct fiscal tools to favor local residents. In particular, the Confederation and the Canton do not allow selective tax reductions for individual companies based on the composition of their workforce. This means the Municipality cannot, for example, reduce a company’s tax burden simply because it hires more Ticinese residents.

However, there are leeway on other fronts. According to the source, potential tools available include:
- Municipal contributions to the training of resident employees;
- Economic incentives for companies hiring resident apprentices;
- Measures to support the professional integration of disadvantaged groups;
- Concessions on municipal services, such as the use of public spaces or the granting of building permits;
- Emphasis on corporate social responsibility criteria when awarding subsidies or public contracts.

📊 > *The Municipality of Chiasso operates within a regulatory framework that leaves little room for maneuver in terms of fiscal incentives. However, alternative tools—such as training contributions or support for apprenticeships—could provide viable solutions to encourage local hiring.*

### Key questions for the Municipality

Romano’s question presents the Chiasso Municipal Council with a series of practical issues. In particular, it asks whether any pilot projects have already been launched or are under evaluation to test these tools. Additionally, it seeks to identify any legal obstacles that could hinder the implementation of more effective measures. For example, cantonal or federal regulations might restrict the possibility of providing direct financial contributions to businesses, or there could be constraints related to state aid under EU law.

💡 > *The challenge for the Municipality lies in balancing legal constraints with the need to support local employment. Exploring non-fiscal incentives and leveraging existing tools could offer a way forward.*

🔍 ⚠️ *Further analysis is needed to assess the feasibility of measures such as subsidies for resident apprenticeships or preferential access to municipal services for businesses prioritizing local hiring.*

Another critical aspect concerns compatibility with international agreements, particularly the Italo-Swiss Convention of 9 December 1976 on cross-border workers. Any measure favouring local residents could indeed be interpreted as indirect discrimination against workers from Lombardy, further complicating dialogue between Ticinese and Italian institutions.

### Border-area businesses: between opportunities and constraints

Chiasso stands as an emblematic case of the cross-border worker phenomenon in Ticino. According to as-yet unspecified data from the source, the municipality hosts a significant number of businesses employing workers from both sides of the border. Most of these companies operate in sectors such as trade, logistics and services, where the flexibility of cross-border workers often proves crucial for competitiveness.

The issue of hiring local residents is therefore intertwined with a series of structural challenges. On one hand, businesses rely on the availability of skilled and flexible labour, which cross-border workers often provide. On the other, local demand for labour remains high, especially in a context of demographic ageing and moderate economic growth.

### What does cantonal law say?

As with other Swiss cantons, Ticino is subject to cantonal and federal regulations governing employment and taxation. In the specific case of Chiasso, the Canton of Ticino has issued several laws and regulations aimed at promoting local employment. However, these tools are often generic and do not target individual municipalities. For example, the canton promotes vocational training programmes and apprenticeship incentives, but these do not include mechanisms for direct preference for residents at the hiring stage.

### The role of social partners

Romano’s question also highlights the role of social partners in the process of promoting local employment. Trade unions and employers' associations could be involved in defining corporate social responsibility criteria, for instance through sectoral agreements that set a minimum quota for local hires. However, the source does not mention any concrete initiatives of this kind currently underway in Chiasso.

In summary, the Municipality of Chiasso faces a complex question: how to reconcile the promotion of local employment with the flexibility needs of border-area businesses, without violating national and international regulations? Francesco Romano’s query to the Chiasso municipal council has sparked a debate on how local policies can shape employment dynamics in a border region like the Mendrisiotto. For cross-border workers living in Lombardy and employed in Chiasso, the issue holds particular significance, as any shift in hiring preferences by local businesses could directly impact their job security.

### Scenario 1: What happens if businesses prefer local residents?

Currently, no official data quantifies the number of cross-border workers employed in Chiasso compared to Ticinese residents. However, it is well-documented that cross-border commuting is a significant phenomenon in southern Ticino, especially in municipalities along the border with Lombardy. If local businesses were to prioritize hiring residents, cross-border workers could face a disadvantage, particularly in sectors where labor demand is not high.

For instance, in retail or hospitality, Ticinese residents might be preferred for permanent positions, while cross-border workers could be hired for part-time or temporary roles. This scenario would reduce demand for cross-border labor, with direct consequences for thousands of Lombard families that rely on Swiss salaries for their livelihood.

> ⚠️ **A note on terminology**: The term *G permit* refers to the cross-border worker permit issued under the Agreement on the Free Movement of Persons between Switzerland and the EU.

### Scenario 2: Impact on tax revenue and local welfare

According to Romano, increased employment of residents in Chiasso would boost tax revenue from individuals domiciled in the municipality. However, this claim requires context. Personal income tax revenue is determined by tax residency, not local employment. A cross-border worker employed in Chiasso but residing in Italy pays taxes in Italy, not Switzerland. Therefore, incentivizing the hiring of residents would not directly increase municipal tax revenue unless those workers also transfer their tax residency to Ticino.

On the other hand, greater local employment could ease pressure on social and public services, as fewer residents would rely on state subsidies. However, the source provides no data on this aspect, making it difficult to assess the potential benefits.

### Non-tax tools: What Chiasso could actually do

Despite regulatory constraints, the Municipality of Chiasso could adopt concrete measures to encourage the hiring of residents. These include:

- Contributions to professional training: The municipal council could fund targeted training courses for residents, in collaboration with SUPSI or other Ticinese institutions. These courses could focus on skills in high demand among local businesses, such as logistics or service sectors.

- Incentives for resident apprentices: A system of bonuses for businesses that hire resident apprentices could be implemented, for example, in the form of subsidies for apprenticeship costs or reductions in municipal taxes.

- Promoting corporate social responsibility: The Municipality could introduce social responsibility criteria in public procurement tenders, rewarding companies that demonstrate a significant commitment to hiring residents. This approach, already tested in some Swiss cantons, could be adapted to the local context.

📊 Key takeaway: Policies aimed at boosting local employment must balance fairness with economic reality, ensuring that cross-border workers are not disproportionately affected by shifts in hiring practices.

- Benefits for municipal services: Businesses hiring local residents could benefit from discounts on fees for the use of public spaces, such as exhibition areas or event venues. Additionally, concessions may be introduced for construction permits, where the hiring of local residents is part of an entrepreneurial project.

### The administrative and legal challenges

Implementing these measures is not without obstacles. First, the Municipality would need to verify compliance with cantonal and federal regulations, particularly those concerning state aid. The Swiss Confederation and the Canton of Ticino could oppose direct financial support to businesses, as such measures might be deemed distortions of competition.

Second, any policy favoring local residents could face challenges under EU law. The Italo-Swiss Convention of 9 December 1976 does not explicitly prohibit local employment promotion policies, but its interpretation could spark controversy. Cross-border workers might file complaints against any indirect discrimination, for example if businesses were incentivized to prioritize local residents for certain jobs.

📊 Legal risks: Any measure favoring residents over cross-border workers could trigger legal disputes under Swiss and EU regulations.

### The case of other border cities: what's happening in Lugano or Bellinzona?

Chiasso is not the only Ticinese municipality addressing the issue of local employment. Similar discussions are underway in Lugano and Bellinzona, albeit in different forms. In Lugano, for instance, the Municipal Council has launched training programs for local residents in collaboration with local businesses, while Bellinzona has introduced social responsibility criteria in public procurement tenders.

However, the lack of concrete data on the composition of the workforce makes it difficult to assess the effectiveness of these measures. The source does not provide direct comparisons between different Ticinese municipalities, but it is clear that the topic of local employment is a priority across the entire canton.

💡 Key takeaway: While policies vary, the focus on local hiring is a shared concern among Ticinese municipalities.

### What impact on cross-border workers?

For cross-border workers living in Lombardy and working in Chiasso, Romano’s inquiry could signal a shift in policy. If the Municipality adopts concrete measures to favor local residents, businesses might be encouraged to prioritize Ticinese workers, especially in sectors with high labor demand.

However, any changes would likely have a gradual impact. Businesses cannot dismiss previously hired cross-border workers without violating Swiss labor laws, which protect employees regardless of nationality. The most probable outcome would be a reduction in new hires for cross-border workers rather than an immediate replacement of those already employed.

📊 Uncertainty remains: Without concrete data, it is impossible to predict the scale of this phenomenon. For example, it is unclear how many businesses in Chiasso predominantly employ cross-border workers or which sectors would be most affected by changes in hiring policies.

⚠️ Potential consequences: While gradual, policy shifts favoring local residents could reduce opportunities for cross-border workers in the long term.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). The Municipality of Chiasso will now have to respond to the question submitted by Francesco Romano, providing a clear overview of the non-tax tools that could be adopted to incentivize the hiring of local residents. But what concrete actions can cross-border workers and companies take today, while awaiting an official response?

### Step 1: Verify tax residency and hiring requirements

First and foremost, it is essential to clarify one’s tax status. Cross-border workers living in Lombardy but working in Chiasso are required to declare their income in Italy, unless specific exemptions apply under the Italo-Swiss Convention of 9 December 1976. However, if a worker is fiscally resident in Ticino, taxes will be withheld in Switzerland. This aspect is crucial, as a change in residency could impact the competitiveness of Ticinese companies in recruiting local workers.

For companies, it is important to review the current hiring criteria and check whether any cantonal programs exist to incentivize the hiring of local residents. For example, the Canton of Ticino promotes apprenticeships as a tool to train young residents, offering contributions to companies that participate in these programs.

### Step 2: Explore training and apprenticeship opportunities

Even in the absence of definitive responses from the Municipality of Chiasso, companies and residents can already benefit from existing tools. SUPSI (University of Applied Sciences and Arts of Italian Switzerland) and USI (University of Italian Switzerland) offer professional training courses in various sectors, including logistics, services, and commerce. These institutions often collaborate with local companies to develop training paths tailored to market needs.

For residents seeking employment, the State Secretariat for Education, Research and Innovation (SEFO) of the Canton of Ticino provides information on apprenticeship and internship opportunities. Additionally, the Department of Economy and Education (DFE) of the Canton of Ticino promotes professional reintegration programs for disadvantaged groups, such as long-term unemployed individuals or people with disabilities.

### Step 3: Monitor municipal and cantonal initiatives

The Municipality of Chiasso may launch pilot projects in the coming months to incentivize the hiring of local residents. It is therefore essential to stay updated on official communications from the Municipality by regularly checking the institutional website or announcements published in the Official Cantonal Gazette. Similarly, the Canton of Ticino could introduce new regional incentives in collaboration with social partners.

An example of an existing initiative is the 'Ticino Lavoro' program, managed by the DFE, which offers free consulting services to companies for recruiting local staff. This service could be expanded in the coming months, especially if the issue of local employment gains further political relevance.

### Step 4: Evaluate municipal service benefits

If the Municipality of Chiasso were to introduce benefits on municipal services for companies that hire local residents, these could include discounts on:
- The use of public spaces for company events;
- The granting of construction permits;
- Participation in public tenders.

📊 Key considerations for cross-border workers and companies:
- Verify tax residency status to avoid double taxation or compliance issues.
- Explore cantonal programs like apprenticeships to align with local workforce development.
- Stay informed about municipal and cantonal initiatives that may introduce new incentives.

These incentives, if implemented, could offer concrete benefits to companies that choose to prioritize local residents. However, as of the time of writing, the specifics of these potential measures have not yet been finalized.

### Step 5: Engaging social partners for support

Trade unions and employers' associations could play a pivotal role in facilitating dialogue between businesses and local residents. For example, the **Swiss Federation for Apprenticeship (FSEA)** or the Ticino Union of SMEs (UTP) could promote sectoral agreements that include minimum quotas for hiring local workers. While these agreements would be voluntary, they could be highly effective in fostering a culture of local employment.

> ⚠️ Key takeaway: Social partners can act as intermediaries to balance the needs of businesses and residents.

### What to do if you're a cross-border worker and fear for your job?

Cross-border workers employed in Chiasso may wonder if they are at risk of being replaced by local Ticino residents. It's important to clarify that, under Swiss law, nationality cannot be a selection criterion for hiring, except in very specific cases permitted by law. Therefore, companies cannot dismiss cross-border workers to hire locals without an objective justification, such as cost reduction or company reorganization.

Nevertheless, it is advisable to closely monitor local job opportunities and assess potential training programs to enhance your competitiveness. Additionally, maintaining a strong relationship with your employer—demonstrating flexibility and willingness to align with company policies—can be beneficial.

> 💡 Pro tip: Proactivity and adaptability are key to securing your position in a competitive job market.

### Useful tools for staying informed

For residents and businesses looking to stay updated on developments, the Canton of Ticino’s website offers several resources:
- [Job search platform](nav:jobs): to browse available job listings and assess demand for local workers in various sectors;
- [Company directory](nav:companies): to find contact details for local businesses and explore collaboration opportunities;
- [Cost of living calculator](nav:cost-of-living): to estimate the additional expenses associated with working and living in Ticino.

### Conclusions and next steps

Francesco Romano’s inquiry to the Municipality of Chiasso represents an attempt to address one of the most sensitive issues for a border town: how to balance the needs of businesses with those of the local population. The Municipality’s response will be closely watched, as it could set the guidelines for future local employment policies in the Mendrisiotto region.

In the meantime, both businesses and residents can already leverage existing tools to improve their positions in the job market. Whether through training, apprenticeships, or municipal incentives, the key will be to stay informed and proactive.

For any questions or need for support, the Frontaliere Ticino team remains available with practical guides and tools to help navigate the complexities of cross-border work.

> 📊 Stay ahead: Knowledge is power—use the right resources to stay competitive.

*Source: [laregione.ch](https://www.laregione.ch/cantone/mendrisiotto/1918689/chiasso-ha-strumenti-per-favorire-l-assunzione-di-residenti)*

item.title: SBB: Direct trains to Rimini and France from 30 May 2026
item.description: From 30 May to 4 October, direct connections will run between Zurich and Rimini, with stops in Bellinzona, Lugano and Chiasso. From Lausanne and Geneva, TGV trains will serve Avignon, Aix-en-Provence and Marseille until 2 November.
item.content:encoded:
The Swiss Federal Railways (SBB CFF FFS) have announced today a series of new direct summer connections to Italy and France, valid from 30 May to 4 October 2026 for the Zurich-Rimini route and from 2 July to 2 November 2026 for French destinations. The new Zurich-Rimini service, running **fourteen times a week**, will stop in Bellinzona, Lugano and Chiasso, offering Ticino cross-border workers and travellers a quick way to reach the Riviera Romagna without passing through Milan.

For those preferring France, SBB CFF FFS has extended TGV connections from Lausanne and Geneva to destinations such as Avignon, Aix-en-Provence and Marseille. These trains will be available from 2 July to 2 November 2026, with daily departures from Thursday to Monday. During July and August, the service will be further enhanced with additional runs on Tuesdays and Wednesdays.

## 📊 Major construction work on the Simplon line and planned cancellations

Summer 2026 will however be marked by significant construction work on the Simplon line, managed by Italian railway infrastructure manager Rete Ferroviaria Italiana (RFI). Between 7 June and 26 July 2026, EuroCity services from Geneva, Lausanne, Bern and Basel to Milan via Domodossola will be cancelled or operate discontinuously. In addition, Regional Express (RE) services between Iselle di Trasquera and Domodossola will be replaced by substitute buses, impacting daily commuters travelling between Ticino and Piedmont.

SBB CFF FFS emphasises that, despite delays caused by the construction work, the introduction of these new connections represents a significant step forward in improving cross-border connectivity, particularly for cross-border workers who cross the Italian-Swiss border every day. The cancellation of direct trains on certain routes calls for careful travel planning, with a need to check updated timetables and consider alternatives such as substitute buses or regional trains.

## 💡 Ticino stops: Lugano, Bellinzona and Chiasso

For residents of Ticino, the stops planned on the new connections represent a real opportunity. Lugano and Bellinzona, the region’s main hubs, will be directly linked with Rimini, while Chiasso, the gateway to Lombardy, will offer a direct connection to the Riviera Romagna. This means that cross-border workers living in the Mendrisiotto or working in the Province of Como can reach Rimini without having to go through Milan, reducing travel times and the hassle of changing trains.

For those heading to France, departures from Lausanne and Geneva provide quick access to tourist destinations such as Avignon or Marseille without the need to change trains in Italian territory. An ideal solution for summer holidays or short stays in Provence.

## ⚠️ What changes compared to 2025

In 2025, direct connections from Zurich to Rimini were limited to a few weekly services, often with intermediate stops in Milan. The novelty of 2026 is the elimination of the Milan transfer, thanks to the new direct route through Ticino. In addition, the extension of TGV services to France represents an enhancement compared to previous years, when options were more limited and often required connections in Italy.

However, the downside is the construction work on the Simplon line, which will force many travellers to adjust their routines. In 2025, cancellations were limited to short periods, but in 2026 the situation will be more challenging, with two months of significant disruptions on the route to Milan.

### Reactions from cross-border workers

Cross-border worker unions have welcomed the introduction of new direct services, but they have expressed concerns about the disruptions caused by the construction work. According to a spokesperson for the Federazione Lavoratori Trasporti Ticinesi (FLTT), *‘The new direct trains to Rimini are a step forward, but the cancellation of Simplon line services risks creating long waits for those who cross the border every day.’* The FLTT has urged travellers to check updated timetables and consider alternative options, such as carpooling or long-distance buses, to avoid delays.

Commuters' associations have also emphasised the importance of clear communication from SBB and RFI. *‘It is essential that information about replacement buses and timetable changes is widely distributed well in advance,’* stated a representative of the Associazione Pendolari Ticinesi (APT). *‘We must avoid leaving travellers unprepared, especially during peak tourist season.’*

### Next steps: what travellers need to do

SBB has already published updated timetables on its official website, but the company recommends checking periodically for changes due to the construction work. Travellers heading to Rimini are advised to book tickets in advance, particularly in July and August when demand is highest. For services to France, seat reservations are mandatory for TGV trains.

Travellers who regularly use the Iselle–Domodossola route will need to use replacement buses, with departures and arrivals from Iselle di Trasquera and Domodossola stations. SBB assures that the buses will be coordinated with regional train timetables, but it is still recommended to arrive at the station at least 30 minutes early to avoid connection issues.

📊 Key advice for cross-border commuters
- Check SBB’s [official website](nav:check_sbb) for real-time updates
- Consider alternative transport options during peak disruption periods
- Book seats early for high-demand routes

💡 Tip for tourists
The new services could be an opportunity to explore Ticino. Stops in Lugano and Bellinzona may encourage travellers to Rimini to spend a day in the region, perhaps connecting to destinations like Locarno or Ascona. Additionally, the direct route to France could attract visitors looking to explore both Italian Switzerland and Provence in a single trip.

⚠️ Important note
Travellers are reminded that the Simplon line disruptions will last for two months in 2026. Plan journeys accordingly and allow extra time for potential delays. For cross-border workers from Ticino and regular travelers between Switzerland and Italy, the new summer schedules introduced by SBB (Swiss Federal Railways) present both opportunities and challenges. While they simplify access to destinations like Rimini or Marseille, the ongoing construction work on the Simplon line requires more rigid planning and the need to consider alternatives to avoid delays.

### Before vs after: how travel is changing

Until 30 May 2026, travelers heading from Zurich to Rimini had to pass through Milano Centrale, with a train change and a route often congested. Now, direct trains are available with stops in Bellinzona, Lugano, and Chiasso, reducing travel time and the hassles associated with connections in Italian territory. For those departing from Lugano or Bellinzona, the advantage is even more evident: it will no longer be necessary to cross the border to reach the Romagna Riviera.

For those heading to France, the changes are equally significant. Previously, connections from Lausanne and Geneva to Avignon or Marseille often required a change in Lyon or Paris, resulting in longer and more complex journeys. Now, with the new direct TGVs, it is possible to reach these destinations without interruptions, saving more than two hours compared to previous options.

### Possible scenarios for cross-border workers

Case 1: Travel to Rimini from Lugano
- Before 30 May 2026: Departure from Lugano, change at Chiasso or Milano Centrale, arrival in Rimini in at least 4 hours (excluding waiting time for the connection).
- After 30 May 2026: Departure from Lugano, direct arrival in Rimini in approximately 5 hours, with intermediate stops in Bellinzona and Zurich. Total travel time reduced, no changes required.

Case 2: Travel to Marseille from Geneva
- Before 2 July 2026: Departure from Geneva, change in Lyon or Paris, arrival in Marseille in 6-7 hours.
- After 2 July 2026: Departure from Geneva, direct arrival in Marseille in approximately 4 hours, with stops in Avignon and Aix-en-Provence.

📊 **Key insights**
> The new schedules significantly improve connectivity for cross-border workers and travelers, reducing travel times and eliminating the need for changes in congested hubs like Milano Centrale.

### Risks: construction work and cancellations

The main uncertainty stems from the construction work on the Simplon line, which between June and July 2026 will result in the cancellation of EuroCity services from Geneva, Lausanne, Bern, and Basel to Milano. For those who regularly use these connections, the advice is to:

- Check updated schedules on the SBB website before traveling.
- Book replacement bus tickets in advance, especially for the Iselle-Domodossola route.
- Consider alternatives such as the Ticino-Lombardy regional train (TILO) or carpooling for short trips.

Another aspect to evaluate is the impact on international connections. For example, travelers heading from Zurich to Rimini after 7 June will have to either take a longer route via Milano or use replacement buses for the Domodossola-Iselle section.

💡 Pro tip
> Always check for real-time updates on construction work and alternative routes, as schedules may change with little notice.

### Impact on Ticino tourism

The new schedules also have implications for tourism in Ticino. Direct connections to popular Italian destinations like Rimini will make it easier for visitors to explore the Romagna Riviera without the hassle of border crossings.
